T1104.name: Multi-Stage Channels
T1104.description:
Adversaries may create multiple stages for command and control that are employed under different conditions or for certain functions. Use of multiple stages may obfuscate the command and control channel to make detection more difficult.

Remote access tools will call back to the first-stage command and control server for instructions. The first stage may have automated capabilities to collect basic host information, update tools, and upload additional files. A second remote access tool (RAT) could be uploaded at that point to redirect the host to the second-stage command and control server. The second stage will likely be more fully featured and allow the adversary to interact with the system through a reverse shell and additional RAT features.

The different stages will likely be hosted separately with no overlapping infrastructure. The loader may also have backup first-stage callbacks or [Fallback Channels](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1008) in case the original first-stage communication path is discovered and blocked.
T1104.mitigations: Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate activity at the network level.
T1104.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detect Multi-Stage Command and Control Channels
- Analytic 'Analytic 0637': Initial process initiates outbound connection to first-stage C2, receives payloads or commands, then spawns or injects into a second process that establishes a new outbound connection to an unrelated destination (second-stage C2).
- Analytic 'Analytic 0638': Shell script or binary initiates curl/wget request to staging domain, writes output to disk or memory, and shortly afterward launches another process that establishes new outbound connection to a different IP or hostname.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0639': Initial process using NSURLSession or similar APIs reaches out to known staging domains, followed by creation of a reverse shell or RAT connecting to a second unrelated server.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0640': CLI-based or API-based network call from the hypervisor to external staging host, shortly followed by a connection to a second external IP by a spawned process or scheduled task.
T1104.procedures:
Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can use one C2 URL for first contact and to upload information about the host computer and two additional C2 URLs for getting commands.
Software 'BLACKCOFFEE': BLACKCOFFEE uses Microsoft’s TechNet Web portal to obtain an encoded tag containing the IP address of a command and control server and then communicates separately with that IP address for C2. If the C2 server is discovered or shut down, the threat actors can update the encoded IP address on TechNet to maintain control of the victims’ machines.
Software 'Valak': Valak can download additional modules and malware capable of using separate C2 channels.
Software 'JumbledPath': JumbledPath can communicate over a unique series of connections to send and retrieve data from exploited devices.
Software 'Bazar': The Bazar loader is used to download and execute the Bazar backdoor.
Software 'Snip3': Snip3 can download and execute additional payloads and modules over separate communication channels.
Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has used a two-tiered C2 configuration with tier one nodes connecting to the victim and tier two nodes connecting to backend infrastructure.
Software 'Uroburos': Individual Uroburos implants can use multiple communication channels based on one of four available modes of operation.
Software 'BACKSPACE': BACKSPACE attempts to avoid detection by checking a first stage command and control server to determine if it should connect to the second stage server, which performs "louder" interactions with the malware.
Software 'Chaos': After initial compromise, Chaos will download a second stage to establish a more permanent presence on the affected system.
Group 'APT3': An APT3 downloader first establishes a SOCKS5 connection to 192.157.198[.]103 using TCP port 1913; once the server response is verified, it then requests a connection to 192.184.60[.]229 on TCP port 81.
Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group has used multi-stage malware components that inject later stages into separate processes.
Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used one C2 to obtain enumeration scripts and monitor web logs, but a different C2 to send data back.
Group 'APT41': APT41 used the storescyncsvc.dll BEACON backdoor to download a secondary backdoor.
Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware with separate channels to request and carry out tasks from C2.
Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware with separate channels to request and carry out tasks from C2.

T1105.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
T1105.description: Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment. Tools or files may be copied from an external adversary-controlled system to the victim network through the command and control channel or through alternate protocols such as [ftp](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0095). Once present, adversaries may also transfer/spread tools between victim devices within a compromised environment (i.e. [Lateral Tool Transfer](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1570)). On Windows, adversaries may use various utilities to download tools, such as `copy`, `finger`, [certutil](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0160), and [PowerShell](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/001) commands such as IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).downloadString() and Invoke-WebRequest. On Linux and macOS systems, a variety of utilities also exist, such as `curl`, `scp`, `sftp`, `tftp`, `rsync`, `finger`, and `wget`.(Citation: t1105_lolbas) A number of these tools, such as `wget`, `curl`, and `scp`, also exist on ESXi. After downloading a file, a threat actor may attempt to verify its integrity by checking its hash value (e.g., via `certutil -hashfile`).(Citation: Google Cloud Threat Intelligence COSCMICENERGY 2023) Adversaries may also abuse installers and package managers, such as `yum` or `winget`, to download tools to victim hosts. Adversaries have also abused file application features, such as the Windows `search-ms` protocol handler, to deliver malicious files to victims through remote file searches invoked by [User Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204) (typically after interacting with [Phishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566) lures).(Citation: T1105: Trellix_search-ms) Files can also be transferred using various [Web Service](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1102)s as well as native or otherwise present tools on the victim system.(Citation: PTSecurity Cobalt Dec 2016) In some cases, adversaries may be able to leverage services that sync between a web-based and an on-premises client, such as Dropbox or OneDrive, to transfer files onto victim systems. For example, by compromising a cloud account and logging into the service's web portal, an adversary may be able to trigger an automatic syncing process that transfers the file onto the victim's machine.(Citation: Dropbox Malware Sync)
T1105.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware or unusual data transfer over known protocols like FTP can be used to mitigate activity at the network level. Signatures are often for unique indicators within protocols and may be based on the specific obfuscation technique used by a particular adversary or tool, and will likely be different across various malware families and versions. Adversaries will likely change tool C2 signatures over time or construct protocols in such a way as to avoid detection by common defensive tools.
Mitigation 'Filter Network Traffic': Use network filtering to block outbound traffic from compromised systems to unapproved external destinations. Restricting access to known, trusted IP addresses and protocols can prevent attackers from downloading malicious tools or payloads onto compromised servers after gaining initial access.
T1105.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detect Ingress Tool Transfers via Behavioral Chain
- Analytic 'Analytic 0165': Unusual or uncommon processes initiate network connections to external destinations followed by file creation (tools downloaded).
- Analytic 'Analytic 0166': Shell-based tools (curl, wget, scp) initiate connections to external domains followed by creation of executable files on disk.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0167': Process execution of curl or wget followed by a network connection and a file created in temporary or user-specific directories.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0168': Command line interface or vCLI triggers remote transfer using wget or curl, writing files into datastore paths or local tmp directories.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0169': Network device logs show anomalous inbound file transfers or uncharacteristic flows with high payload volume to network devices with storage or automation hooks.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell
T1059.001.description: Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution. PowerShell is a powerful interactive command-line interface and scripting environment included in the Windows operating system.(Citation: TechNet PowerShell) Adversaries can use PowerShell to perform a number of actions, including discovery of information and execution of code. Examples include the Start-Process cmdlet which can be used to run an executable and the Invoke-Command cmdlet which runs a command locally or on a remote computer (though administrator permissions are required to use PowerShell to connect to remote systems). PowerShell may also be used to download and run executables from the Internet, which can be executed from disk or in memory without touching disk. A number of PowerShell-based offensive testing tools are available, including [Empire](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0363), [PowerSploit](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0194), [PoshC2](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0378), and PSAttack.(Citation: Github PSAttack) PowerShell commands/scripts can also be executed without directly invoking the powershell.exe binary through interfaces to PowerShell's underlying System.Management.Automation assembly DLL exposed through the .NET framework and Windows Common Language Interface (CLI).(Citation: Sixdub PowerPick Jan 2016)(Citation: SilentBreak Offensive PS Dec 2015)(Citation: Microsoft PSfromCsharp APR 2014)
T1059.001.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Disable or Remove Feature or Program': It may be possible to remove PowerShell from systems when not needed, but a review should be performed to assess the impact to an environment, since it could be in use for many legitimate purposes and administrative functions. Disable/restrict the WinRM Service to help prevent uses of PowerShell for remote execution.
Mitigation 'Antivirus/Antimalware': Anti-virus can be used to automatically quarantine suspicious files.
Mitigation 'Code Signing': Set PowerShell execution policy to execute only signed scripts.
Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': When PowerShell is necessary, consider restricting PowerShell execution policy to administrators. Be aware that there are methods of bypassing the PowerShell execution policy, depending on environment configuration. PowerShell JEA (Just Enough Administration) may also be used to sandbox administration and limit what commands admins/users can execute through remote PowerShell sessions.
Mitigation 'Execution Prevention': Use application control where appropriate. PowerShell Constrained Language mode can be used to restrict access to sensitive or otherwise dangerous language elements such as those used to execute arbitrary Windows APIs or files (e.g., `Add-Type`).
T1059.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Abuse of PowerShell for Arbitrary Execution
- Analytic 'Analytic 1252': Detects behavioral chains where PowerShell is launched with encoded commands, unusual parent processes, or suspicious modules loaded, potentially followed by network connections or child process spawning. Supports detection of both direct (powershell.exe) and indirect (.NET automation) invocations.
T1059.001.procedures: Software 'Empire': Empire leverages PowerShell for the majority of its client-side agent tasks. Empire also contains the ability to conduct PowerShell remoting with the Invoke-PSRemoting module. Software 'Covenant': Covenant can create PowerShell-based launchers for Grunt installation. Software 'Pupy': Pupy has a module for loading and executing PowerShell scripts. Software 'Koadic': Koadic has used PowerShell to establish persistence. Software 'Sliver': Sliver has built-in functionality to launch a Powershell command prompt. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can use PowerShell to execute commands. Software 'ConnectWise': ConnectWise can be used to execute PowerShell commands on target machines. Software 'BloodHound': BloodHound can use PowerShell to pull Active Directory information from the target environment. Software 'Donut': Donut can generate shellcode outputs that execute via PowerShell. Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can execute PowerShell commands via WMI. Software 'PowerSploit': PowerSploit modules are written in and executed via PowerShell. Software 'AADInternals': AADInternals is written and executed via PowerShell. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub can use PowerShell to delete volume shadow copies. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH can utilize `powershell.exe` to execute commands on a compromised host. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda uses PowerShell to download and execute the payload. Software 'SystemBC': SystemBC has used hidden scheduled tasks to execute PowerShell commands by adding the following: `-WindowStyle Hidden -ep bypass -file `. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can use PowerShell to download and execute payloads. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed has the ability to execute its payload via PowerShell. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb has the ability to run shell commands via PowerShell. Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can use PowerShell to apply Group Policy changes. Software 'Spica': Spica can use an obfuscated PowerShell command to create a scheduled task for persistence. Software 'REvil': REvil has used PowerShell to delete volume shadow copies and download files. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can execute a PowerShell script received from C2. Software 'TRANSLATEXT': TRANSLATEXT has used PowerShell to collect system information and to upload the collected data to a Github repository. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can execute PowerShell commands and scripts with the use of .NET DLL, `WoodyPowerSession`. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can execute PowerShell scripts received from C2. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can use PowerShell to support multiple actions including execution and defense evasion. Software 'PowerStallion': PowerStallion uses PowerShell loops to iteratively check for available commands in its OneDrive C2 server. Software 'JCry': JCry has used PowerShell to execute payloads. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot has been known to use PowerShell to download new payloads, open documents, and upload data to command and control servers. Software 'LazyWiper': LazyWiper has used PowerShell to enable data destruction on targeted systems. Software 'MoleNet': MoleNet can use PowerShell to set persistence. Software 'Akira': Akira will execute PowerShell commands to delete system volume shadow copies. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman can execute PowerShell commands and has used PowerShell to execute a keylogger. Software 'GLASSTOKEN': GLASSTOKEN can use PowerShell for command execution. Software 'PowerShower': PowerShower is a backdoor written in PowerShell. Software 'LitePower': LitePower can use a PowerShell script to execute commands. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can execute PowerShell commands on a compromised machine. Software 'Troll Stealer': Troll Stealer creates and executes a PowerShell script to delete itself. Software 'NETWIRE': The NETWIRE binary has been executed via PowerShell script. Software 'ShrinkLocker': ShrinkLocker uses PowerShell to disable protectors used to secure the BitLocker encryption key on victim machines and then delete the key from the system. Software 'PowerPunch': PowerPunch has the ability to execute through PowerShell. Software 'Havoc': Havoc can facilitate the execution of PowerShell commands. Software 'Gootloader': Gootloader can use an encoded PowerShell stager to write to the Registry for persistence. Software 'POWERTON': POWERTON is written in PowerShell. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer variants have used PowerShell scripts to download or drop payloads, including obfuscated variants to connect to a WebDAV server to download and executed an encrypted DLL for installation. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito can launch PowerShell Scripts. Software 'Hancitor': Hancitor has used PowerShell to execute commands. Software 'Cuba': Cuba has been dropped onto systems and used for lateral movement via obfuscated PowerShell scripts. Software 'Helminth': One version of Helminth uses a PowerShell script. Software 'PS1': PS1 can utilize a PowerShell loader. Software 'Pysa': Pysa has used Powershell scripts to deploy its ransomware. T1055.004.name: Asynchronous Procedure Call
T1055.004.description: Adversaries may inject malicious code into processes via the asynchronous procedure call (APC) queue in order to evade process-based defenses as well as possibly elevate privileges. APC injection is a method of executing arbitrary code in the address space of a separate live process. APC injection is commonly performed by attaching malicious code to the APC Queue (Citation: Microsoft APC) of a process's thread. Queued APC functions are executed when the thread enters an alterable state.(Citation: Microsoft APC) A handle to an existing victim process is first created with native Windows API calls such as OpenThread. At this point QueueUserAPC can be used to invoke a function (such as LoadLibrayA pointing to a malicious DLL). A variation of APC injection, dubbed "Early Bird injection", involves creating a suspended process in which malicious code can be written and executed before the process' entry point (and potentially subsequent anti-malware hooks) via an APC. (Citation: CyberBit Early Bird Apr 2018) AtomBombing (Citation: ENSIL AtomBombing Oct 2016) is another variation that utilizes APCs to invoke malicious code previously written to the global atom table.(Citation: Microsoft Atom Table) Running code in the context of another process may allow access to the process's memory, system/network resources, and possibly elevated privileges. Execution via APC injection may also evade detection from security products since the execution is masked under a legitimate process.
T1055.004.mitigations: Mitigation 'Behavior Prevention on Endpoint': Some endpoint security solutions can be configured to block some types of process injection based on common sequences of behavior that occur during the injection process.
T1055.004.detections:
Detection Strategy: Behavioral Detection of Asynchronous Procedure Call (APC) Injection via Remote Thread Queuing
- Analytic 'Analytic 0277': Detects malicious injection behavior involving memory allocation, remote thread queuing via APC (e.g., QueueUserAPC), and altered thread context within another live process to execute unauthorized code under legitimate context.
T1055.004.procedures: Software 'TURNEDUP': TURNEDUP is capable of injecting code into the APC queue of a created Rundll32 process as part of an "Early Bird injection." Software 'HeartCrypt': HeartCrypt has the ability to use `NtQueueApcThread` as an alternate method for process injection. Software 'Pillowmint': Pillowmint has used the NtQueueApcThread syscall to inject code into svchost.exe. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can inject its code into a trusted process via the APC queue. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can use asynchronous procedure call (APC) injection to execute commands received from C2. Software 'Saint Bot': Saint Bot has written its payload into a newly-created `EhStorAuthn.exe` process using `ZwWriteVirtualMemory` and executed it using `NtQueueApcThread` and `ZwAlertResumeThread`. Software 'Carberp': Carberp has queued an APC routine to explorer.exe by calling ZwQueueApcThread. Software 'IcedID': IcedID has used ZwQueueApcThread to inject itself into remote processes. Software 'XLoader': XLoader injects code into the APC queue using `NtQueueApcThread` API. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH can inject itself into a new `svchost.exe -k netsvcs` process using the asynchronous procedure call (APC) queue. Software 'Attor': Attor performs the injection by attaching its code into the APC queue using NtQueueApcThread API. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic can use the `QueueUserAPC` API to execute shellcode on a compromised machine. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has injected malicious code into a new svchost.exe process.

T1016.name: System Network Configuration Discovery
T1016.description: Adversaries may look for details about the network configuration and settings, such as IP and/or MAC addresses, of systems they access or through information discovery of remote systems. Several operating system administration utilities exist that can be used to gather this information. Examples include [Arp](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0099), [ipconfig](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0100)/[ifconfig](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0101), [nbtstat](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0102), and [route](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0103). Adversaries may also leverage a [Network Device CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/008) on network devices to gather information about configurations and settings, such as IP addresses of configured interfaces and static/dynamic routes (e.g. show ip route, show ip interface).(Citation: US-CERT-TA18-106A)(Citation: Mandiant APT41 Global Intrusion ) On ESXi, adversaries may leverage esxcli to gather network configuration information. For example, the command `esxcli network nic list` will retrieve the MAC address, while `esxcli network ip interface ipv4 get` will retrieve the local IPv4 address.(Citation: Trellix Rnasomhouse 2024) Adversaries may use the information from [System Network Configuration Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1016) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including determining certain access within the target network and what actions to do next.
T1016.detections:
Detection Strategy: Behavioral Detection of System Network Configuration Discovery
- Analytic 'Analytic 0559': Execution of built-in tools (e.g., ipconfig, route, netsh) or PowerShell/WMI queries to enumerate IP, MAC, interface status, or routing configuration.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0560': Execution of `ifconfig`, `ip a`, or access to `/proc/net/` indicating collection of local interface and route configuration.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0561': Execution of `ifconfig`, `networksetup`, or `system_profiler` to query IP/MAC/interface configuration and status.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0562': Use of `esxcli network` commands (e.g., `esxcli network nic list`, `esxcli network ip interface ipv4 get`) via SSH or hostd to enumerate adapter and IP information.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0563': CLI-based execution of interface and routing discovery commands (e.g., `show ip interface`, `show arp`, `show route`) over Telnet, SSH, or console.
T1016.procedures: Software 'ifconfig': ifconfig can be used to display adapter configuration on Unix systems, including information for TCP/IP, DNS, and DHCP. Software 'Koadic': Koadic can retrieve the contents of the IP routing table as well as information about the Windows domain. Software 'Empire': Empire can acquire network configuration information like DNS servers, public IP, and network proxies used by a host. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can enumerate network adapter information. Software 'NBTscan': NBTscan can be used to collect MAC addresses. Software 'PcShare': PcShare can obtain the proxy settings of a compromised machine using `InternetQueryOptionA` and its IP address by running `nslookup myip.opendns.comresolver1.opendns.com\r\n`. Software 'Pupy': Pupy has built in commands to identify a host’s IP address and find out other network configuration settings by viewing connected sessions. Software 'AdFind': AdFind can extract subnet information from Active Directory. Software 'Sliver': Sliver has the ability to gather network configuration information. Software 'nbtstat': nbtstat can be used to discover local NetBIOS domain names. Software 'QuasarRAT': QuasarRAT has the ability to enumerate the Wide Area Network (WAN) IP through requests to ip-api[.]com, freegeoip[.]net, or api[.]ipify[.]org observed with user-agent string `Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 6.3; rv:48.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/48.0`. Software 'ipconfig': ipconfig can be used to display adapter configuration on Windows systems, including information for TCP/IP, DNS, and DHCP. Software 'ShimRatReporter': ShimRatReporter gathered the local proxy, domain, IP, routing tables, mac address, gateway, DNS servers, and DHCP status information from an infected host. Software 'route': route can be used to discover routing configuration information. Software 'evilginx2': evilginx2 can capture information from each session with a victim including the public IP used to access the server and the user agent. Software 'Arp': Arp can be used to display ARP configuration information on the host. Software 'Nltest': Nltest may be used to enumerate the parent domain of a local machine using /parentdomain. Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can collect DNS information from the targeted system. Software 'AsyncRAT': AsyncRAT can enumerate the NetBIOS name on targeted machines. Software 'Explosive': Explosive has collected the MAC address from the victim's machine. Software 'Chrommme': Chrommme can enumerate the IP address of a compromised host. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet collects the IP address of a compromised system. Software 'Dyre': Dyre has the ability to identify network settings on a compromised host. Software 'Olympic Destroyer': Olympic Destroyer uses API calls to enumerate the infected system's ARP table. Software 'Pikabot': Pikabot gathers victim network information through commands such as ipconfig and ipconfig /all. Software 'T9000': T9000 gathers and beacons the MAC and IP addresses during installation. Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can collect the IP address of a compromised host. Software 'IceApple': The IceApple ifconfig module can iterate over all network interfaces on the host and retrieve the name, description, MAC address, DNS suffix, DNS servers, gateways, IPv4 addresses, and subnet masks. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can collect IP information from the victim’s machine. Software 'VERMIN': VERMIN gathers the local IP address. Software 'KeyBoy': KeyBoy can determine the public or WAN IP address for the system. Software 'POWRUNER': POWRUNER may collect network configuration data by running ipconfig /all on a victim. Software 'PowerDuke': PowerDuke has a command to get the victim's domain and NetBIOS name. Software 'Sagerunex': Sagerunex will gather system information such as MAC and IP addresses. Software 'KEYMARBLE': KEYMARBLE gathers the MAC address of the victim’s machine. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can determine the NetBios name and the IP addresses of targets machines including domain controllers. Software 'NanoCore': NanoCore gathers the IP address from the victim’s machine. Software 'Pysa': Pysa can perform network reconnaissance using the Advanced IP Scanner tool. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can use ipconfig and Arp to collect network configuration information, including routing information and ARP tables. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic has the ability to execute the `ipconfig` command. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT collects the victim IP address, MAC address, as well as the victim account domain name. Software 'JHUHUGIT': A JHUHUGIT variant gathers network interface card information. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can discover the IP and MAC address of a targeted host. Software 'iKitten': iKitten will look for the current IP address. Software 'Emissary': Emissary has the capability to execute the command ipconfig /all. Software 'PlugX': PlugX has captured victim IP address details of the targeted machine. Software 'Kevin': Kevin can collect the MAC address and other information from a victim machine using `ipconfig/all`. Software 'Saint Bot': Saint Bot can collect the IP address of a victim machine. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D can collect the network interface MAC address on the infected host. Software 'KONNI': KONNI can collect the IP address from the victim’s machine. Software 'Rifdoor': Rifdoor has the ability to identify the IP address of the compromised host. Software 'JPIN': JPIN can obtain network information, including DNS, IP, and proxies. Software 'Troll Stealer': Troll Stealer collects the MAC address of victim devices. Software 'OceanSalt': OceanSalt can collect the victim’s IP address. Software 'LunarLoader': LunarLoader can verify the targeted host's DNS name which is then used in the creation of a decyrption key. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed can identify the IP of a targeted system. Software 'Agent Tesla': Agent Tesla can collect the IP address of the victim machine and spawn instances of netsh.exe to enumerate wireless settings. Software 'Milan': Milan can run `C:\Windows\system32\cmd.exe /c cmd /c ipconfig /all 2>&1` to discover network settings. Software 'LiteDuke': LiteDuke has the ability to discover the proxy configuration of Firefox and/or Opera. Software 'Elise': Elise executes ipconfig /all after initial communication is made to the remote server. Software 'Calisto': Calisto runs the ifconfig command to obtain the IP address from the victim’s machine. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity can identify the IP address of a compromised host. Software 'Caterpillar WebShell': Caterpillar WebShell can gather the IP address from the victim's machine using the IP config command. Software 'Okrum': Okrum can collect network information, including the host IP address, DNS, and proxy information. Software 'Mis-Type': Mis-Type may create a file containing the results of the command cmd.exe /c ipconfig /all. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to identify the location, public IP address, and domain name on a compromised host. Software 'Manjusaka': Manjusaka gathers information about current network connections, local and remote addresses associated with them, and associated processes. Software 'RogueRobin': RogueRobin gathers the IP address and domain from the victim’s machine. Software 'FatDuke': FatDuke can identify the MAC address on the target computer. Software 'MacMa': MacMa can collect IP addresses from a compromised host. Software 'NanHaiShu': NanHaiShu can gather information about the victim proxy server. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can retrieve IP addresses of compromised machines. Software 'Prikormka': A module in Prikormka collects information from the victim about its IP addresses and MAC addresses. Software 'J-magic': J-magic can compare the host and remote IPs to check if a received packet is from the infected machine. Software 'Penquin': Penquin can report the IP of the compromised host to attacker controlled infrastructure. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent can enumerate the IP and domain of a target system. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor has collected the MAC address of a compromised host; it can also use GetAdaptersInfo to identify network adapters. Software 'Pisloader': Pisloader has a command to collect the victim's IP address. Software 'GoldMax': GoldMax retrieved a list of the system's network interface after execution. Software 'ZeroT': ZeroT gathers the victim's IP address and domain information, and then sends it to its C2 server. Software 'PLAINTEE': PLAINTEE uses the ipconfig /all command to gather the victim’s IP address. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can use shell commands to discover network adapters and configuration. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT has the ability to collect the MAC address of an infected host. Software 'Small Sieve': Small Sieve can obtain the IP address of a victim host. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe enumerates the IP address, network proxy settings, and domain name from a victim's system. Software 'Kobalos': Kobalos can record the IP address of the target machine. Software 'Machete': Machete collects the MAC address of the target computer and other network configuration information. Software 'Octopus': Octopus can collect the host IP address from the victim’s machine. Software 'QUADAGENT': QUADAGENT gathers the current domain the victim system belongs to. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has obtained information about local networks through the `ipconfig /all` command. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST collected all network interface MAC addresses that are up and not loopback devices, as well as IP address, DHCP configuration, and domain information. Software 'SpeakUp': SpeakUp uses the ifconfig -a command. Software 'USBferry': USBferry can detect the infected machine's network topology using ipconfig and arp. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can use net config workstation, arp -a, `nslookup`, and ipconfig /all to gather network configuration information. Software 'zwShell': zwShell can obtain the victim IP address. Software 'FELIXROOT': FELIXROOT collects information about the network including the IP address and DHCP server. Software 'FALLCHILL': FALLCHILL collects MAC address and local IP address information from the victim. Software 'Conti': Conti can retrieve the ARP cache from the local system by using the GetIpNetTable() API call and check to ensure IP addresses it connects to are for local, non-Internet, systems. Software 'EKANS': EKANS can determine the domain of a compromised host. Software 'MagicRAT': MagicRAT collects system network information using commands such as `ipconfig /all`. Software 'Unknown Logger': Unknown Logger can obtain information about the victim's IP address. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has used the GetAdaptersInfo() API call to get the victim's MAC address. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can retrieve the ARP cache from the local system by using GetIpNetTable. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy runs the ipconfig /all command. Software 'SHARPSTATS': SHARPSTATS has the ability to identify the domain of the compromised host. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can parse the `ProxyServer` string in the Registry to discover http proxies. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole gathers information on the IP forwarding table, MAC address, configured proxy, and network SSID. Software 'Avenger': Avenger can identify the domain of the compromised host. Software 'UPPERCUT': UPPERCUT has the capability to gather the victim's proxy information. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has leveraged webservices to identify the public IP of the victim host. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer’s 61850 payload component enumerates connected network adapters and their corresponding IP addresses. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can accept a command line argument identifying specific IPs. Software 'WellMail': WellMail can identify the IP address of the victim system. Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette can identify the IP of a compromised system. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke collects information on network settings and Internet proxy settings from the victim. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has collected the victim machine's local IP address information and MAC address. Software 'Royal': Royal can enumerate IP addresses using `GetIpAddrTable`. Software 'CreepySnail': CreepySnail can use `getmac` and `Get-NetIPAddress` to enumerate network settings. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth collects the external IP address from the system. Software 'cd00r': cd00r can discover the IP for the network interface on the compromised device. Software 'ShadowPad': ShadowPad has collected the domain name of the victim system. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer can enumeate information about victims’ systems including IP addresses. Software 'LoudMiner': LoudMiner used a script to gather the IP address of the infected machine before sending to the C2. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can enumerate network information on compromised hosts. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink can use the Linux API `if_nameindex` to gather network interface names. Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has leveraged server-side client configurations to identify the public IP of the victim host. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has collected the local IP address, and external IP. Software 'QUIETCANARY': QUIETCANARY can identify the default proxy setting on a compromised host. Software 'Green Lambert': Green Lambert can obtain proxy information from a victim's machine using system environment variables. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA gathers the victim’s IP address via the ipconfig -all command. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains a command to collect the victim MAC address and LAN IP. Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS can retrieve IP, network adapter configuration information, and domain from compromised hosts. Software 'KOPILUWAK': KOPILUWAK can use Arp to discover a target's network configuration setttings. Software 'Valak': Valak has the ability to identify the domain and the MAC and IP addresses of an infected machine. Software 'Revenge RAT': Revenge RAT collects the IP address and MAC address from the system. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to identify the MAC address on an infected host. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist has the ability to collect the domain name on a compromised host. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron gathers information about network adapters using the Win32 API call GetAdaptersInfo. Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE enumerates network interfaces on the infected host. Software 'Sykipot': Sykipot may use ipconfig /all to gather system network configuration details. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can enumerate infected system network information. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves can obtain information about network parameters. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can use the `GetAdaptersInfo` function to retrieve information about network adapters and the `GetIpNetTable` function to retrieve the IPv4 to physical network address mapping table. Software 'SDBbot': SDBbot has the ability to determine the domain name and whether a proxy is configured on a compromised host. Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang can collect the TCP/IP, DNS, DHCP, and network adapter configuration on a compromised host via ipconfig.exe /all. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer can gather the IP address from the victim's machine. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can collect the IP address and NetBIOS name of an infected machine. Software 'Dtrack': Dtrack can collect the host's IP addresses using the ipconfig command. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can enumerate the MAC address of the compromised host. Software 'Turian': Turian can retrieve the internal IP address of a compromised host. Software 'Catchamas': Catchamas gathers the Mac address, IP address, and the network adapter information from the victim’s machine. Software 'Diavol': Diavol can enumerate victims' local and external IPs when registering with C2. Software 'S-Type': S-Type has used `ipconfig /all` on a compromised host. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito uses the ipconfig command. Software 'PowerShower': PowerShower has the ability to identify the current Windows domain of the infected host. Software 'Gootloader': Gootloader can use an embedded script to check the IP address of potential victims visiting compromised websites. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can identify the IP address and user domain on the target machine. Software 'DEADEYE': DEADEYE can discover the DNS domain name of a targeted system. Software 'Nightdoor': Nightdoor gathers information on victim system network configuration such as MAC addresses. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has a command to get the public IP address from a system. Software 'Trojan.Karagany': Trojan.Karagany can gather information on the network configuration of a compromised host. Software 'MoonWind': MoonWind obtains the victim IP address. Software 'Gomir': Gomir collects network information on infected systems such as listing interface names, MAC and IP addresses, and IPv6 addresses. Software 'Orz': Orz can gather victim proxy information. Software 'Felismus': Felismus collects the victim LAN IP address and sends it to the C2 server. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower has the ability to use ipconfig to enumerate system network settings. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can retrieve network interface and proxy information. Software 'NGLite': NGLite identifies the victim system MAC and IPv4 addresses and uses these to establish a victim identifier. Software 'BoxCaon': BoxCaon can collect the victim's MAC address by using the GetAdaptersInfo API. Software 'Epic': Epic uses the nbtstat -n and nbtstat -s commands on the victim’s machine. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has the ability to identify the IP of the infected host. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can determine the IP and physical location of the compromised host via IPinfo. Software 'BabyShark': BabyShark has executed the ipconfig /all command. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot has the ability to discover the domain name of the infected host. Software 'Proxysvc': Proxysvc collects the network adapter information and domain/username information based on current remote sessions. Software 'OSInfo': OSInfo discovers the current domain information. Software 'Azorult': Azorult can collect host IP information from the victim’s machine. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can collected the IP address and domain name of a compromised host. Software 'Duqu': The reconnaissance modules used with Duqu can collect information on network configuration. Software 'Agent.btz': Agent.btz collects the network adapter’s IP and MAC address as well as IP addresses of the network adapter’s default gateway, primary/secondary WINS, DHCP, and DNS servers, and saves them into a log file. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to identify the MAC address of a compromised host. Software 'Lizar': Lizar has retrieved network information from a compromised host, such as the MAC address. Software 'BlackEnergy': BlackEnergy has gathered information about network IP configurations using ipconfig.exe and about routing tables using route.exe. Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon can collect the external IP address of the victim. Software 'Pay2Key': Pay2Key can identify the IP and MAC addresses of the compromised host. Software 'NOKKI': NOKKI can gather information on the victim IP address. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal can execute ipconfig on the victim’s machine. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar gathers information about network adapters. Software 'Clambling': Clambling can enumerate the IP address of a compromised machine. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon can collect and send the local IP address, RDP information, and the network adapter physical address as a part of its C2 beacon. Software 'Ryuk': Ryuk has called GetIpNetTable in attempt to identify all mounted drives and hosts that have Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) entries. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can gather victim internal and external IPs. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to identify the IP address of the compromised machine. Software 'Havoc': Havoc has a module for network enumeration including determining IP addresses. Software 'Xbash': Xbash can collect IP addresses and local intranet information from a victim’s machine. Software 'Torisma': Torisma can collect the local MAC address using `GetAdaptersInfo` as well as the system's IP address. Software 'Reaver': Reaver collects the victim's IP address. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot obtains the IP address, location, and other relevant network information from the victim’s machine. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun can detect network adapter and IP address information. Software 'BADCALL': BADCALL collects the network adapter information. Software 'yty': yty runs ipconfig /all and collects the domain name. Software 'SocGholish': SocGholish has the ability to enumerate the domain name of a victim, as well as if the host is a member of an Active Directory domain. Software 'Squirrelwaffle': Squirrelwaffle has collected the victim’s external IP address. Software 'Amadey': Amadey can identify the IP address of a victim machine. Group 'HEXANE': HEXANE has used Ping and `tracert` for network discovery. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has used ipconfig and arp to determine network configuration information. Mustang Panda has also utilized SharpNBTScan to scan the victim environment. Group 'Play': Play has used the information-stealing tool Grixba to enumerate network information. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte used tools such as Arp to pull system network information and identify connected devices. Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has used batch scripts to enumerate network information, including information about trusts, zones, and the domain. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following command after exploiting a machine with LOWBALL malware to acquire information about local networks: ipconfig /all >> %temp%\download Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used commands such as `ipconfig` and `netstat` to gather network information on compromised hosts. Group 'Moses Staff': Moses Staff has collected the domain name of a compromised network. Group 'APT19': APT19 used an HTTP malware variant and a Port 22 malware variant to collect the MAC address and IP address from the victim’s machine. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has used `nslookup` and `ipconfig` for network reconnaissance efforts. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has executed multiple commands to enumerate network topology and settings including `ipconfig`, `netsh interface firewall show all`, and `netsh interface portproxy show all`.Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity gathers victim IP information during initial installation stages. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group malware IndiaIndia obtains and sends to its C2 server information about the first network interface card’s configuration, including IP address, gateways, subnet mask, DHCP information, and whether WINS is available. Group 'APT3': A keylogging tool used by APT3 gathers network information from the victim, including the MAC address, IP address, WINS, DHCP server, and gateway. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware to collect the victim’s IP address and domain name. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used several tools to scan for open NetBIOS nameservers and enumerate NetBIOS sessions. Group 'SideCopy': SideCopy has identified the IP address of a compromised host. Group 'Stealth Falcon': Stealth Falcon malware gathers the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) table from the victim. Group 'APT1': APT1 used the ipconfig /all command to gather network configuration information. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has used scripts to collect the host's network topology. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 actors use NBTscan to discover vulnerable systems. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang has performed local network configuration discovery using ipconfig. Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider has used network reconnaissance commands for discovery including `ping` and `nltest`. Group 'Naikon': Naikon uses commands such as netsh interface show to discover network interface settings. Group 'APT42': APT42 has used malware, such as GHAMBAR and POWERPOST, to collect network information. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used ipconfig for reconnaissance.During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace used Arp and `dir` for discovery in compromised environments. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used ipconfig, Ping, and tracert to enumerate the IP address and network environment and settings of the local host. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound malware gathers the victim's local IP address, MAC address, and external IP address. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used malware to collect information on network interfaces, including the MAC address. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover network configuration details using the arp -a, nbtstat -n, net config, ipconfig /all, and route commands, as well as NBTscan. Turla RPC backdoors have also retrieved registered RPC interface information from process memory. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used ipconfig to identify the network configuration of a victim machine. Wizard Spider has also used the PowerShell cmdlet `Get-ADComputer` to collect IP address data from Active Directory. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has enumerated the host machine’s IP address. Group 'APT41': APT41 collected MAC addresses from victim machines. During C0017, APT41 used `cmd.exe /c ping %userdomain%` for discovery. Group 'GALLIUM': GALLIUM used ipconfig /all to obtain information about the victim network configuration. The group also ran a modified version of NBTscan to identify available NetBIOS name servers. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet has gathered information on victim network configuration. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used `ipconfig/all` and web beacons sent via email to gather network configuration information. Kimsuky has also identified Host IP addresses leveraging the WMI class `Win32_NetworkAdapterConfiguration`. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has collected the IP address and network adapter information from the victim’s machine. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has collected IP information via IPInfo. Group 'APT32': APT32 used the ipconfig /all command to gather the IP address from the system. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used ipconfig to gather network configuration information. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has run ipconfig /all on a victim. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca used the command ipconfig to obtain information about network configurations. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has obtained host network details utilizing the command `cmd.exe /c ipconfig /all`. Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used a tool to enumerate proxy settings in the target environment. Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 leveraged JunoOS CLI queries to obtain the interface index which contains system and network details. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used `ipconfig`, `nbtstat`, `tracert`, `route print`, and `cat /etc/hosts` commands. Campaign 'C0017': During C0017, APT41 used `cmd.exe /c ping %userdomain%` for discovery. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity gathers victim IP information during initial installation stages. Campaign 'Operation AkaiRyū': During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace used Arp and `dir` for discovery in compromised environments. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries gathered network configuration details utilizing `arp -a` and `nslookup` commands. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 leveraged JunoOS CLI queries to obtain the interface index which contains system and network details. Campaign 'FunnyDream': During FunnyDream, the threat actors used ipconfig for discovery on remote systems. Campaign 'Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign': During the Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign, the adversary configured Claude Code to identify and gather system configurations of discovered devices. Campaign 'C0018': During C0018, the threat actors ran `nslookup` and Advanced IP Scanner on the target network. Campaign 'ShadowRay': During ShadowRay, threat actors invoked DNS queries from targeted machines to identify their IP addresses. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors used Empire to find the public IP address of a compromised system. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors discovered the local network configuration with `ipconfig`. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors used code to obtain the external public-facing IPv4 address of the compromised host.

T1210.name: Exploitation of Remote Services
T1210.description:
Adversaries may exploit remote services to gain unauthorized access to internal systems once inside of a network. Exploitation of a software vulnerability occurs when an adversary takes advantage of a programming error in a program, service, or within the operating system software or kernel itself to execute adversary-controlled code. A common goal for post-compromise exploitation of remote services is for lateral movement to enable access to a remote system.

An adversary may need to determine if the remote system is in a vulnerable state, which may be done through [Network Service Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1046) or other Discovery methods looking for common, vulnerable software that may be deployed in the network, the lack of certain patches that may indicate vulnerabilities,  or security software that may be used to detect or contain remote exploitation. Servers are likely a high value target for lateral movement exploitation, but endpoint systems may also be at risk if they provide an advantage or access to additional resources.

There are several well-known vulnerabilities that exist in common services such as SMB(Citation: CIS Multiple SMB Vulnerabilities) and RDP(Citation: NVD CVE-2017-0176) as well as applications that may be used within internal networks such as MySQL(Citation: NVD CVE-2016-6662) and web server services.(Citation: NVD CVE-2014-7169)(Citation: Ars Technica VMWare Code Execution Vulnerability 2021) Additionally, there have been a number of vulnerabilities in VMware vCenter installations, which may enable threat actors to move laterally from the compromised vCenter server to virtual machines or even to ESXi hypervisors.(Citation: Broadcom VMSA-2024-0019)

Depending on the permissions level of the vulnerable remote service an adversary may achieve [Exploitation for Privilege Escalation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1068) as a result of lateral movement exploitation as well.
T1210.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Disable or Remove Feature or Program': Minimize available services to only those that are necessary.
Mitigation 'Vulnerability Scanning': Regularly scan the internal network for available services to identify new and potentially vulnerable services.
Mitigation 'Exploit Protection': Security applications that look for behavior used during exploitation such as Windows Defender Exploit Guard (WDEG) and the Enhanced Mitigation Experience Toolkit (EMET) can be used to mitigate some exploitation behavior. Control flow integrity checking is another way to potentially identify and stop a software exploit from occurring. Many of these protections depend on the architecture and target application binary for compatibility and may not work for all software or services targeted.
Mitigation 'Network Segmentation': Segment networks and systems appropriately to reduce access to critical systems and services to controlled methods.
Mitigation 'Threat Intelligence Program': Develop a robust cyber threat intelligence capability to determine what types and levels of threat may use software exploits and 0-days against a particular organization.
Mitigation 'Application Isolation and Sandboxing': Make it difficult for adversaries to advance their operation through exploitation of undiscovered or unpatched vulnerabilities by using sandboxing. Other types of virtualization and application microsegmentation may also mitigate the impact of some types of exploitation. Risks of additional exploits and weaknesses in these systems may still exist.
Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': Minimize permissions and access for service accounts to limit impact of exploitation.
Mitigation 'Update Software': Update software regularly by employing patch management for internal enterprise endpoints and servers.
T1210.detections:
Detection Strategy: Exploitation of Remote Services – multi-platform lateral movement detection
- Analytic 'Analytic 0327': Correlates inbound network access to remote service ports (e.g., SMB/RPC 445/135, RDP 3389, WinRM 5985/5986) with near-time instability in the target service (crash, abnormal restart), suspicious child process creation under the service, and post-access lateral-movement behaviors. The chain indicates likely exploitation rather than normal administration.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0328': Links inbound network access to SSHD/SMB/NFS/Databases or custom daemons with subsequent daemon crash/restart, core dump, or spawning of shells/reverse shells from the service context, indicating remote exploitation.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0329': Detects exploitation targeting ESXi/vCenter by correlating attempts to reach known exploitable endpoints (OpenSLP 427, CIM 5989, Hostd/Vpxa HTTPS 443, ESXi SOAP) with vmkernel/hostd crashes, unexpected hostd/vpxa restarts, or new reverse/outbound connections from ESXi host/vCenter to internal assets.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0330': Ties inbound access to exposed services (ARD/VNC 5900, SSH 22, ScreenSharing, web services) with process crashes in unified logs and abnormal child processes spawned under those services (e.g., bash, curl) to indicate exploitation.
T1210.procedures:
Software 'Empire': Empire has a limited number of built-in modules for exploiting remote SMB, JBoss, and Jenkins servers.
Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 contains a module for exploiting SMB via EternalBlue.
Software 'Flame': Flame can use MS10-061 to exploit a print spooler vulnerability in a remote system with a shared printer in order to move laterally.
Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry uses an exploit in SMBv1 to spread itself to other remote systems on a network.
Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet propagates using the MS10-061 Print Spooler and MS08-067 Windows Server Service vulnerabilities.
Software 'QakBot': QakBot can move laterally using worm-like functionality through exploitation of SMB.
Software 'Emotet': Emotet has been seen exploiting SMB via a vulnerability exploit like EternalBlue (MS17-010) to achieve lateral movement and propagation.
Software 'Bad Rabbit': Bad Rabbit used the EternalRomance SMB exploit to spread through victim networks.
Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya can use two exploits in SMBv1, EternalBlue and EternalRomance, to spread itself to other remote systems on the network.
Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can spread within a network via the BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) and EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) vulnerabilities in RDP and SMB respectively.
Software 'Conficker': Conficker exploited the MS08-067 Windows vulnerability for remote code execution through a crafted RPC request.
Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can exploit multiple vulnerabilities including EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) and EternalRomance (CVE-2017-0144).
Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot utilizes EternalBlue and EternalRomance exploits for lateral movement in the modules wormwinDll, wormDll, mwormDll, nwormDll, tabDll.
Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has exploited or attempted to exploit Zerologon (CVE-2020-1472) and EternalBlue (MS17-010) vulnerabilities.
Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has exploited known vulnerabilities in remote services including RDP.
Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used Mimikatz to exploit a domain controller via the ZeroLogon exploit (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear has used exploits for vulnerabilities such as MS17-010, also known as `Eternal Blue`, during operations.
Group 'APT28': APT28 exploited a Windows SMB Remote Code Execution Vulnerability to conduct lateral movement.
Group 'Tonto Team': Tonto Team has used EternalBlue exploits for lateral movement.
Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used tools to exploit the ZeroLogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has exploited a Windows Netlogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472) to obtain access to Windows Active Directory servers.
Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has exploited the Microsoft Netlogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 has exploited MS17-010 to move laterally to other systems on the network.
Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has exploited ZeroLogon (CVE-2020-1472) against vulnerable domain controllers.

CVE-2020-14750.title: Oracle WebLogic Server Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
CVE-2020-14750.description: Vulnerability in the Oracle WebLogic Server product of Oracle Fusion Middleware (component: Console). Supported versions that are affected are 10.3.6.0.0, 12.1.3.0.0, 12.2.1.3.0, 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.1.0.0. Easily exploitable vulnerability allows unauthenticated attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise Oracle WebLogic Server. Successful attacks of this vulnerability can result in takeover of Oracle WebLogic Server. CVSS 3.1 Base Score 9.8 (Confidentiality, Integrity and Availability impacts). CVSS Vector: (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H).
CVE-2020-14750.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2020-14882.title: Oracle WebLogic Server Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
CVE-2020-14882.description: Vulnerability in the Oracle WebLogic Server product of Oracle Fusion Middleware (component: Console). Supported versions that are affected are 10.3.6.0.0, 12.1.3.0.0, 12.2.1.3.0, 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.1.0.0. Easily exploitable vulnerability allows unauthenticated attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise Oracle WebLogic Server. Successful attacks of this vulnerability can result in takeover of Oracle WebLogic Server. CVSS 3.1 Base Score 9.8 (Confidentiality, Integrity and Availability impacts). CVSS Vector: (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H).
CVE-2020-14882.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2015-4852.title: Oracle WebLogic Server Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability
CVE-2015-4852.description: The WLS Security component in Oracle WebLogic Server 10.3.6.0, 12.1.2.0, 12.1.3.0, and 12.2.1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands via a crafted serialized Java object in T3 protocol traffic to TCP port 7001, related to oracle_common/modules/com.bea.core.apache.commons.collections.jar. NOTE: the scope of this CVE is limited to the WebLogic Server product.
CVE-2015-4852.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.
